coredata.dc:title: Multimodal synchronous monitoring platform for state of charge stratified thermal runaway in lithium iron phosphate batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Han, Longfei
Zhang, Mengdan
Hu, Xiangming
Yang, Xinyue
Li, Jinfeng

Han, Guoyu
Jiang, Lihua
Deng, Yurui
Cheng, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: As a critical component in electric vehicles and energy storage systems, the dynamic relationship between state of charge (SOC) and thermal runaway (TR) propagation in LiFePO4 batteries remains insufficiently understood. To address the critical limitation of existing TR testing methods in achieving synchronized multiparameter acquisition, this study developed an integrated multi-physics monitoring platform enabling spatiotemporal correlation analysis across the entire TR chain-from triggered initiation, heat/smoke release, gas speciation, 2 dimension temperature field reconstruction (infrared thermography), to TR process visualization. Systematic investigation of 18650-type LiFePO4 cells across SOC gradients revealed distinct failure modes: 100% SOC cells exhibited predominant heat-driven failure with total heat release reaching 5.95 MJ/m² (a 5-fold increase versus 50% SOC cells), while 50% SOC cells demonstrated prioritized smoke aerosol release (520% higher particulate density than 100% SOC) with delayed combustible gas generation. This platform overcomes single-parameter detection constraints in conventional methods, providing multiscale experimental evidence to guide SOC-stratified safety protocols and phase-change thermal barrier material optimization for lithium-ion battery systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synchronous monitoring platform
Lithium-ion battery
LiFePO4
Thermal runaway
State of charge

Parameters analysis of different SOC batteries

Dr. L.H. Jiang is supported by the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province (tsqn202408191).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different thermal runaway test methods.
Combustion phenomena and infrared thermography images of LFP cells at different SOC: (a) 0% SOC; (b) 50% SOC; (c) 100% SOC.

Time versus temperature curves, temperature rise rate curves, and XRD of the material after TR for LFP batteries with different SOC: (a) 0% SOC; (b) 50% SOC; (c) 100% SOC; (d) XRD plots of the anode of LFP batteries with different SOC after heating; (e) a magnified section of the plot in (b); (f) a magnified section of the plot in (c).
Analysis of thermal and flue gas changes during TR of LFP with different SOC: (a) HRR; (b) THR; (c) SPR; (d) CO release rate; (e) CO2 release rate; (f) Total Smoke Release (TSR).
Mapping of elements O, P and Fe in different cathode: (a) 0% SOC; (b) 50% SOC; (c) 100% SOC.
XPS maps of substances after TR test of LFP batteries with different SOC: (a) O 1s; (b) P 2p; (c) Fe 2p; (d) F 1s.
Radar diagram of various parameters of LFP battery in different SOC.

head.ce:title: Multimodal synchronous monitoring platform for state of charge stratified thermal runaway in lithium iron phosphate batteries

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Safety and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a critical component in electric vehicles and energy storage systems, the dynamic relationship between state of charge (SOC) and thermal runaway (TR) propagation in LiFePO4 batteries remains insufficiently understood. To address the critical limitation of existing TR testing methods in achieving synchronized multiparameter acquisition, this study developed an integrated multi-physics monitoring platform enabling spatiotemporal correlation analysis across the entire TR chain-from triggered initiation, heat/smoke release, gas speciation, 2 dimension temperature field reconstruction (infrared thermography), to TR process visualization. Systematic investigation of 18650-type LiFePO4 cells across SOC gradients revealed distinct failure modes: 100% SOC cells exhibited predominant heat-driven failure with total heat release reaching 5.95 MJ/m² (a 5-fold increase versus 50% SOC cells), while 50% SOC cells demonstrated prioritized smoke aerosol release (520% higher particulate density than 100% SOC) with delayed combustible gas generation. This platform overcomes single-parameter detection constraints in conventional methods, providing multiscale experimental evidence to guide SOC-stratified safety protocols and phase-change thermal barrier material optimization for lithium-ion battery systems.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalating global energy demand, coupled with mounting environmental concerns, has driven the rapid adoption of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) as pivotal energy storage technologies due to their high efficiency and eco-friendly attributes [1,2]. Among LIBs variants, lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO₄ or LFP) batteries have emerged as a prominent candidate for applications in electric vehicles and grid-scale energy storage systems, owing to their high energy density, extended cycle life, and cost-effectiveness [3–7]. Despite these advantages, LFP batteries are not immune to critical safety challenges, particularly thermal runaway (TR)—a self-sustaining exothermic reaction chain that induces catastrophic temperature spikes, structural degradation, and potential combustion or explosion [8–10]. TR is often triggered by complex electrochemical-thermal interactions, which accelerate decomposition of battery components, release hazardous gases, and compromise system integrity [11–13]. A series of chain reactions occur during the TR process in batteries [14,15]. Initially, a temperature leads to increase the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) membrane [16]. Followed by a reaction between the electrolyte and the cathode, producing a small amount of gas. With further temperature escalation, the battery’s anode material begins to decompose, causing a violent reaction of the electrolyte with both electrodes [17]. This results in the release of significant heat and gas. Simultaneously, the separator begins to contract and dissolve, leading to an internal short circuit within the battery. Ultimately, the separator completely melts, causing the cathode and anode to make direct contact. The electrolyte undergoes vigorous decomposition, resulting in battery ignition or explosion [18–20].

A critical factor influencing TR behavior is the state of charge (SOC), which governs the electrochemical activity and thermal stability of the battery [21–24]. Studies demonstrate that elevated SOC levels exacerbate TR severity, significantly altering key parameters such as triggering temperature, heat generation, and gas emissions [25–29]. At high SOC, the cathode and anode inside the battery exhibit higher reactivity. As the battery temperature rises, the electrodes become more prone to side reactions, leading to an earlier onset of thermal runaway and amplified heat release rates (HRR) [30,31]. These SOC-dependent dynamics underscore the urgent need to elucidate the interplay between electrochemical states and TR mechanisms to develop robust safety protocols.

To address this gap, this study proposes an innovative experimental methodology for comprehensively investigating TR behavior in LFP batteries across varying SOC levels [28]. The approach integrates multi-modal, real-time monitoring to capture dynamic thermal, and physical responses during TR progression. Thermal analysis was conducted via cone calorimetry, enabling precise quantification of critical parameters such as HRR and total heat release (THR). Synchronized thermocouple arrays provided high-resolution temperature profiles, revealing heat transfer mechanisms at micro- and macroscopic scales. Gas evolution dynamics were simultaneously analyzed using the cone calorimeter’s integrated gas sampling system, which characterized the composition and temporal release patterns of volatile species. External high-definition imaging documented structural deformation and gas ejection phenomena, offering visual insights into morphological changes during TR. Post-TR characterization of solid residues via spectroscopic and microscopic techniques further clarified decomposition pathways and reaction byproducts.

By unifying these diagnostic tools, this work establishes a novel platform for evaluating TR safety, bridging the disconnect between isolated thermal, gaseous, and material analyses in existing studies. The methodology not only advances fundamental understanding of SOC-dependent TR mechanisms but also provides actionable data for designing fail-safe battery systems. This holistic approach sets a precedent for multi-physics investigations of battery failure modes, offering critical insights for risk mitigation strategies in next-generation energy storage technologies.

A custom experimental apparatus ( Fig. 1) was designed to simulate and analyze TR under controlled conditions. The setup integrates various techniques to comprehensively and accurately simulate and monitor all aspects of battery behavior during the TR process [32].

The system primarily heats the battery using a heating plate to simulate the triggering process of TR by an external heat source [33]. In the experimental configuration, the battery was heated primarily by a heating plate to simulate the external heat source-induced TR process. The battery base was positioned in close contact with the heating plate to ensure efficient heat transfer. The heating protocol consisted of two stages: the plate temperature was first raised from room temperature at a rate of 5 °C/min to 150 °C, followed by an increased heating rate of 10 °C/min until TR occurred. The heating plate operated within a temperature range of room temperature to 300 °C, with a temperature control accuracy of ±1 °C. Real-time temperature monitoring of the heating plate was conducted via a temperature sensor, with the heating rate adjusted by the control system to ensure compliance with the pre-set thermal protocol. During the experiment, the battery undergoes thermal decomposition, producing carbon dioxide (CO2), carbon monoxide (CO) and other gases. A cone calorimeter is used to measure the gas concentrations generated during combustion, the rate of smoke generation, and the HRR [34]. Additionally, thermocouples continuously monitor the temperature changes during the TR process [35]. To document the combustion behavior of the battery during TR, a high-definition camera is set up outside. It can clearly capture changes of flame shape, spread speed, and key moments such as the deformation and rupture of the battery. An infrared thermal imaging device is also used to conduct an all-around scan of the battery’s surface, generating a high-resolution temperature distribution map.

Fig. 2 summarizes the commonly used methods for testing battery TR reported in the literature [36–39]. Accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC), while widely adopted for its precision in measuring HRR and THR during TR [40], but exhibiting inherent limitations in gas release and smoke analysis. Specifically, ARC lacks dedicated sensors for quantifying gas species, concentrations, or smoke particulate characteristics [41,42]. Similarly, situ calorimeter can record the voltage changes of the battery during the TR process, which can reflect the changes in the battery’s state. Also, it can also measure the mass loss of the battery during the reaction, which is related to the decomposition and gas release of the battery materials. However, this method pays limited attention to the release of gases and smoke [43].

Combustion chamber-based analytical methods typically utilize gas sensors and spectroscopic techniques to detect volatile compounds. It is often failed to capture spatially resolved temperature gradients within the battery, thereby restricting mechanistic insights into TR initiation and propagation [23,27]. Nail penetration tests, designed to simulate TR triggered by mechanical abuse, focus primarily on temperature and voltage responses during internal short circuits. While effective for evaluating failure modes under mechanical stress, this method offers minimal data on HRR, THR, or gas composition during runaway events [44,45].

In contrast, the integrated experimental platform developed in this study addresses these limitations by enabling synchronized multi-parameter analysis. The system simultaneously quantifies (i) surface and internal temperature distributions via thermocouple arrays and infrared imaging, (ii) HRR and THR through cone calorimetry, (iii) gas emission kinetics (CO, CO2, hydrocarbons) and smoke particulate dynamics, and (iv) morphological changes via high-speed imaging. This holistic approach facilitates cross-dimensional correlation of thermal, electrochemical, and gas-phase behaviors, providing unprecedented resolution for TR mechanism elucidation. By bridging gaps in existing methodologies, the platform delivers comprehensive datasets critical for advancing predictive models and safety-optimized battery designs.

Commercial Soshine 18650 LiFePO₄ (LFP) batteries with a nominal capacity of 1600 mAh were employed in this study. Prior to testing, all batteries underwent 10 full charge-discharge cycles at 0.5 C (800 mA) to stabilize their electrochemical performance. To prepare samples at distinct SOC, the batteries were discharged to 0% SOC using a constant current (CC) of 0.5 C. Subsequent charging was performed under CC mode to achieve 50% and 100% SOC levels. Each SOC condition was evaluated sequentially using identical testing protocols. To ensure statistical reliability, triplicate experiments were conducted for every SOC level, and data were systematically analyzed to compare performance variations across SOCs.

Thermal and gas release behaviors during TR were monitored using a cone calorimeter (TESTech, Suzhou, China), which quantified critical parameters including HRR, THR, smoke production rate (SPR), and gas concentrations (e.g., CO, CO₂). Temperature profiles during TR were recorded using K-type thermocouples (±1 °C accuracy), while a high-definition camera (1080 p, 60 fps) and infrared thermal imager (FLIR, 0.1 °C resolution) captured visual and thermal dynamics. Post-TR material characterization was performed via X-ray diffraction (XRD; Rigaku Co., Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.15418 nm), scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi PHILIPS XL30E, 3 kV beam energy), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; VG ESCALAB MK-II, Al Kα excitation source) [34].

Fig. 3 illustrates the combustion behavior and infrared thermal imaging profiles of LiFePO₄ (LFP) batteries during TR at varying SOC. For the 0% SOC battery (Fig. 3a), TR progression is characterized by sluggish reactivity: the safety valve opens at 294 s, releasing minimal smoke (Fig. S1a), with no significant exothermic escalation observed during prolonged heating. Thermal imaging reveals a peak temperature of 323 °C at 768 s, indicating subdued TR severity. In contrast, the 50% SOC battery (Fig. 3b) exhibits heightened reactivity, with safety valve activation at 291 s followed by substantial smoke emission at 371 s (Fig. S1b). Despite the absence of open flames, thermal imaging records a maximum temperature of 392 °C, reflecting intensified heat generation compared to the 0% SOC case.

Notably, the 100% SOC battery (Fig. 3c) undergoes rapid TR onset, marked by initial smoke release at 138 s (Fig. S1c) and violent flame eruption at 368 s. The reaction culminates in a peak temperature of 648 °C—nearly double that of lower SOC conditions. These results demonstrate a clear SOC-dependent escalation in TR dynamics: higher SOC correlates with accelerated reaction kinetics, amplified heat release, and increased propensity for flame formation. Thermal imaging further corroborates this trend, showing a progressive rise in maximum temperatures (323 °C → 392 °C → 648 °C) with increasing SOC. Collectively, the data underscore SOC as a critical determinant of TR intensity, providing mechanistic insights into failure thresholds under operational conditions.

Fig. 4 delineates the TR progression of LFP batteries across varying SOC levels through time-temperature profiles and temperature rise rate (dT/dt) curves, revealing 3 distinct TR phases. Stage I (initial phase) features gradual battery surface temperature escalation accompanied by minor smoke emission (Fig. S1), attributed to the decomposition of the SEI layer. This degradation eliminates the passivation barrier between the electrode and electrolyte, enabling direct interfacial contact and initiating subsequent parasitic reactions, including electrolyte oxidation and accelerated lithium-ion depletion. Stage II (accelerated phase) is marked by safety valve activation and exponential temperature surges, with SOC-dependent variations in reaction intensity. The 0% SOC battery exhibits a peak temperature rise rate of 1.02 °C/s (Fig. 4a), culminating at 323 °C. At 50% SOC (Fig. 4b), the rate escalates to 16.05 °C/s, generating substantial smoke and a 392 °C peak. The 100% SOC battery (Fig. 4c) demonstrates extreme kinetics, achieving a 79.4 °C/s rise rate (5 times faster than 50% SOC) and 648 °C maximum temperature, alongside violent flames and dense smoke. Stage III (extinguishing phase) follows rapid energy depletion, characterized by diminishing combustion, temperature decline, and smoke reduction. As a large amount of energy is released inside the battery during the TR process, the combustible materials are also nearly exhausted. The burning and heat generation of the battery gradually weaken. Consequently, the temperature starts to decrease gradually, flames are extinguished, and the smoke is significantly reduced in stage III.

Following the completion of the reaction, the battery’s temperature gradually decreased to room temperature. Then, the heated battery was photographed (Fig S2). The images reveal significant changes in the appearance of the three types of batteries. The 0% SOC battery (Fig S2a) did not experience intense combustion. However, its surface exhibited noticeable deformation and discoloration. The original metallic sheen of the casing had faded to a gray state. The 50% SOC battery (Fig S2b) showed more severe shell deformation. Numerous marks from high-temperature exposure were visible. Some areas even developed tiny holes, likely caused by the rapid expansion of internal gases during the TR acceleration stage. The 100% SOC battery (Fig S2c) suffered the most severe damage. With the entire battery nearly completely deformed. The shell extensively ruptured and peeled, exhibiting extreme carbonization. This observation illustrates that the violent reactions experienced by high SOC batteries during TR result in destructive damage.

Fig. 4d further investigate the internal changes, the battery was disassembled and XRD tests were conducted. This indicates that the cathode material undergoes thermal decomposition upon heating [46]. For the 0% SOC LFP battery, only C-Graphite is produced. This suggests that a relatively lower degree of thermal decomposition at low SOC. No other carbon-related substances are formed. In contrast, the 50% SOC and 100% SOC LFP batteries generate C-Graphite as well as additional carbon-related substances (C₈-Carbon) following TR. This indicates that at higher SOC, the degree of thermal decomposition of the cathode material is greater. This results in producing more carbon-related substances. Therefore, the higher the SOC of a LFP battery, the more unstable the LFP material becomes, thereby increasing the battery’s potential hazard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of heat and gas generation in batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 provides a systematic comparison of TR behaviors in LFP batteries at varying states of charge (SOC: 0%, 50%, 100%) through cone calorimetry analysis, quantifying heat release, smoke production, and gas evolution dynamics.

As shown in Fig. 5a, the 100% SOC battery initiates intense heat release at 362 s, peaking at 550 kW/m² by 369 s, while negligible HRR variations are observed for 0% and 50% SOC cells (Fig. S3). THR profiles (Fig. 5b) further highlight SOC-dependent escalation: the 100% SOC battery achieves a maximum THR of 5.95 MJ/m² at 440 s, whereas the 50% SOC cell exhibits delayed THR elevation (onset at 361 s), and the 0% SOC sample shows minimal thermal activity.

Smoke production dynamics (Fig. 5c) reveal a stark contrast: the 50% SOC battery demonstrates the highest smoke production rate (SPR), correlating with incomplete combustion, while the 100% SOC cell generates substantial smoke due to rapid combustion completeness. Gas emission analysis (Figs. 5d‐e) confirms accelerated CO and CO2 release rates for the 100% SOC battery, peaking at 386 s and 375 s, respectively. In contrast, the 50% SOC cell emits trace gases, and the 0% SOC battery produces no detectable CO/CO2, consistent with its non-combustive TR behavior. The above results indicates that as the SOC increases, the combustion of the battery becomes more complete, resulting in a higher quantity of CO and CO2 gases produced.

Notably, TSR values (Fig. 5f) exhibit an inverse trend: the 100% SOC battery registers a TSR of 6.35 m²/m², while the 50% SOC cell shows a 520% increase (33.03 m²/m²). In the context of 100% SOC batteries, the paradox between high THR and low smoke production is fundamentally attributed to the combustion completeness disparity. At full charge, the battery’s internal active materials reach maximum availability, leading to extremely intense exothermic reactions during TR. The THR reaches 5.95 MJ/m², 5-fold higher than that of 50% SOC cells-with a peak temperature of 648 °C, sufficient to fully decompose organic components into CO and CO2. This results in high combustion efficiency, with minimal generation of unburned carbon particulates or liquid-phase aerosols. By contrast, 50% SOC cells exhibit incomplete combustion at a peak temperature of 392 °C, leading to the release of substantial unburned carbon residues-including particulate matter and polymer fragments-as smoke. The particulate density in 50% SOC cells is 520% higher than that in 100% SOC cells, confirming the role of incomplete combustion in smoke generation. XRD and SEM analyses reveal that 100% SOC triggers significantly accelerated decomposition of LiFePO4 and graphite at the positive electrode, whereas 50% SOC retains residual solid materials that contribute to smoke formation during TR. These findings highlight SOC as a critical modulator of TR severity by governing the interplay between heat release, gas evolution, and combustion efficiency. Therefore, it is evident that battery under 0% SOC, undergoing no combustion or rupture, produces negligible smoke. These findings collectively underscore SOC as a critical modulator of TR severity, governing heat-gas-smoke interplay and combustion efficiency.

Post-TR electrode degradation was systematically analyzed via SEM and elemental mapping to elucidate SOC-dependent structural and compositional evolution. For the 0% SOC battery, the LFP cathode exhibits surface cracks macroscopically (Fig. S4a), yet high-magnification SEM (Fig. S4b, d) reveals preserved particle integrity. Elemental mapping ( Fig. 6a) confirms homogeneous distributions of O, P, and Fe, indicating minimal oxidative decomposition. Conversely, the graphite anode displays partial cracking and coarse particulates (Fig. S5), suggesting mechanical stress-induced degradation despite low SOC.

At 50% SOC, the LFP cathode develops pronounced surface irregularities and microcracks (Fig. S6a) and high magnification reveals that the particle structure is partially altered at elevated temperatures (Figs. S6b, d). As shown in Fig. 6b, the distribution of Fe element on the surface of the cathode is more uniform, indicating that the cathode has a certain pyrolysis, and the damage of LFP particles is more obvious. The SEM image of the graphite anode surface (Fig. S7) shows agglomerated particles. Under magnification, the graphite layer appears significantly broken and fragmented into smaller pieces.

LFP battery exhibits significant slate-like morphology at high temperature as shown in Fig. S8. Upon magnification, serious structural breakage and pronounced particle agglomeration are observed. Elemental analysis of the surface in Fig. 6c shows an increase in O content and a substantial decrease in Fe content. These observations confirm that the LFP cathode undergoes severe oxidative degradation and structural disintegration under thermal stress, while the graphite anode demonstrates pronounced particle agglomeration, crack propagation, and eventual collapse of its layered architecture (Fig. S9).

By comparing and analyzing the SEM and mapping images of the anodes and cathodes at various SOC states. It was observed that at 0% SOC battery, the electrode structure is relatively intact, with more uniform elemental distribution. In contrast, at 100% SOC battery, the electrodes are severely damaged. It exhibits non-uniform elemental distribution and an uneven surface structure. This suggests that a higher SOC in LFP batteries increases the risk of TR and results in more severe damage post TR.

Fig. 7 delineates the SOC-dependent chemical evolution of LFP battery material post-TR through XPS analysis of O 1s, P 2p, Fe 2p, and F 1s core levels, supplemented by survey spectra in Figs. S10(a-c). In Fig. 7a, the O 1s spectrum is divided into 2 binding peaks at 532.1 eV and 533.7 eV. These 2 peaks represent the pyrolysis phenomena of the electrolyte and the separator. The P 2p energy spectrum (Fig. 7b) reveals 2 binding peaks at 133.8 eV and 134.5 eV, which primarily represent the LFP after TR.

The XPS results in Fig. 7c indicate that the Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ peaks of the thermal runaway products show negligible variations across different SOCs, demonstrating the high structural stability of the LiFePO₄ cathode material that remains essentially unchanged during thermal runaway. In Fig. 7d, the F 1s spectrum is divided into 2 binding peaks at 685.3 eV and 687.1 eV. As the SOC increases, the proportion of the C-F/P-F peak area increase. This indicates that SOC exhibits minimal influence on cathode stability. Therefore, it can be seen that the state of charge has impact on the chemical state of the materials in LFP batteries after TR. At different SOC levels, the chemical environment, forms, and content of elements such as O, P, Fe, and F vary.

In Fig. S10a, the total spectrum of the 0% SOC battery shows distinct C 1s and O 1s peaks. This indicates a significant presence of C and O in the battery materials. This is consistent with the fact that most battery electrode materials and electrolytes contain a large of carbon-based materials and oxygen-containing compounds. Additionally, the P 2p, F 2p, and Fe 2p peaks also show some intensity. This suggests the presence of P, F, and Fe elements in the battery materials, which come from the electrode active materials and electrolyte additives, among others. At this point, the intensity of each element’s peak reflects the chemical composition of the battery in an uncharged state. In Fig. S10b the total spectrum of the battery at 50% SOC shows that its overall peak shape is similar to that the 0% SOC battery [47]. The total spectrum of 100% SOC in Fig. S10c shows the different elements on the surface of the electrode material at a high SOC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters analysis of different SOC batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 synthesizes the SOC-dependent escalation of TR severity in LFP batteries through radar plots comparing 6 critical parameters maximum value: maximum temperature (T max), total heat release (THR max), smoke production rate (SPR max) and the peak value of heat release rate (P HRR), total smoke release (P TSR), and carbon monoxide/dioxide production rates (P CO, P CO₂).

Under 0% SOC, the battery exhibits minimal TR activity, with all parameters at baseline levels (T max = 323 °C, THR max = 0.29 MJ/m², P HRR = 8.8 kW/m²). Limited electrochemical energy storage suppresses exothermic decomposition pathways, resulting in negligible gas and smoke emissions. This state represents a low-risk regime, meaning that even if reactions occur, the amount of released heat is quite limited.

The 50% SOC battery shows a significant upward trend in several parameters compared to the battery at 0% SOC. The increases in THR max and P HRR indicate that as the energy state of the active materials inside the battery increases. Both the intensity of thermal reactions and the rate of energy release are also rising. The production rates of P CO and P CO2 have also increased. This suggests that a certain level of chemical reactions is beginning to occur inside the battery. The possible decomposition of some active materials. This reflects that although the battery has not yet reached the most dangerous state at 50% SOC, the risk of TR has already started to increase.

When the battery’s SOC reaches 100%, almost all parameters reach their maximum values. Specifically, T max is close to 700 °C, THR max is approximately 6 MJ/m2, and P TSR is about 6.35 m2/m2. This indicates that in a fully charged state, the active materials inside the battery are in a high energy state. Even minor external stimuli or internal abnormalities can trigger intense thermal reactions. The high production rates of P CO and P CO2 suggest that severe decomposition reactions are occurring inside the battery. Additionally, a high TSR may indicate rapid changes in the microstructure of battery materials, which further exacerbate heat transfer and reaction processes. This greatly increases the probability and severity of TR.

Overall, the SOC significantly influences the TR characteristics of a battery. As the SOC increases from 0% to 100%, the related TR parameters also rise, leading to a marked increase in the intensity and hazard of TR. This effect occurs because higher SOC levels correspond to greater stored chemical energy within the battery. When TR is triggered, this additional energy contributes to higher temperatures, increased heat release, and greater production of gas and smoke.

This study systematically examines LFP battery thermal safety across diverse SOC levels, using a multi-dimensional diagnostic platform that combines cone calorimetry, thermocouples, gas analysis, and post-TR material characterization to analyze TR mechanisms in thermal, chemical, and physical terms. Key findings indicate that SOC significantly affects TR severity. High SOC accelerates reaction kinetics, shortens TR onset, and increases hazard intensity. At 100% SOC, batteries show extreme temperatures (648 °C), peak heat release (550 kW/m²), severe electrode damage, and high CO/CO₂ emissions. At 50% SOC, there's a slight reduction in thermal and gas emissions, but more smoke due to incomplete combustion. At 0% SOC, TR is minimal, with the least heat/gas release and better structural integrity. This research offers crucial theoretical support for optimizing battery thermal management systems. It also broadens the safe and efficient use of lithium - energy in various fields and is highly significant for enhancing the stability and reliability of new energy systems.

Longfei Han: Methodology, Visualization, Data analysis. Mengdan Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiangming Hu: Supervision, Resources. Xinyue Yang: Simulation, Data analysis and Writing − original draft. Jinfeng Li: Data analysis. Xiaoxuan Wei: Data analysis. Guoyu Han: Writing − original draft. Lihua Jiang: Funding acquisition. Yurui Deng: Supervision. Yuan Cheng: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by preparation of MOF/aramid separator with sustained-release lithium nitrate and study on the mitigation mechanism of battery thermal runaway supported by Qingdao Natural Science Foundation (25-1-1-87-zyyd-jch), National Natural Science Foundation of China (52304245), National Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2023QE167) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52204248). Dr. L.H. Jiang is supported by the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province (tsqn202408191).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nxener.2025.100398.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Warning lithium-ion battery thermal runaway with 4-min relaxation voltage [J]
Appl. Energy
